meta.title: Protection against rabies in mice by a cytotoxic T cell clone recognizing the glycoprotein of rabies virus -- Kawano et al. 71 (2): 281 -- Journal of General Virology

abstractHtml: By the use of liposomes containing the purified surface glycoprotein (G) of rabies virus and the haemagglutinin-neuraminidase (HN) and fusion (F) glycoproteins of Sendai virus, the target antigen of anti- rabies virus cytotoxic T lymphocyte (CTL) clones isolated in a previous study was identified as the G protein. Recognition of the H-2K determinant of the class I major histocompatibility complex (MHC) was necessary for target lysis by the CTL clones. One of the CTL clones was examined for the ability to protect mice against a lethal rabies virus infection. CTL were transferred into syngeneic mice which had been infected in the hind footpad with the ERA strain of rabies virus. The infection was converted into a lethal infection by cyclophosphamide treatment 1 day after virus infection. Transfer of CTL 2 to 3 days after virus infection protected approximately 50% of mice during the observation period of 4 weeks. Greater protection was obtained in mice receiving both anti-rabies virus antibodies and CTL cells.
